question: Explain the composition of the DHRS simplified diagram.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters for both primary and secondary systems, such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures. These parameters are calculated using thermal hydraulic software and verified manually, with uncertainties and design margins considered to ensure efficient operation and safety. The design supports natural circulation with minimal pressure losses, and uncertainties related to steam generator tube plugging do not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components in the NuScale reactor, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments. It references relevant sections of the BPVC (Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code) and lists the applicable material specifications for components such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits. The DHRS's reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated, and it operates independently or alongside the Emergency Core Cooling System if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is responsible for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary to prevent radionuclide release. Located inside the containment vessel, the RCS uses natural circulation to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generators, with flow driven by coolant density differences and elevation changes. The system interfaces with several other subsystems, including emergency cooling and chemical control systems.

nuscale_ch05_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch05_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifically Chapter Five on the Reactor Coolant System and Connecting Systems. It specifies that only the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to use, copy, or disclose the information for regulatory purposes, and any copies must include the copyright notice. The section also references the table of contents for Chapter Five, which covers the summary description and design basis of the reactor coolant system.

nuscale_ch05_0021.title: Reactor Coolant System Piping
nuscale_ch05_0021.summary: The section discusses the design and degradation assessment of the NuScale reactor coolant system piping, specifically focusing on steam generator (SG) tubing. It notes that the main degradation observed in similar operating reactors is wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects, and that NuScale's novel helical tube and support design does not increase risk compared to existing designs. Prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines are deemed sufficient, with wear remaining the primary concern and secondary side corrosion mechanisms identified as low risk due to slow defect growth rates.

edges.description: Section A references Section B by directing the reader to Section 5.4 for further description of the steam generators, which are components relevant to the DHRS simplified diagram.

Section A references the connection of the DHRS to the steam and feed piping and points to Section B for further description of the SGs, while Section B provides the design basis and function of the DHRS, thus Section A introduces and cross-references the more detailed technical content in Section B.

Section A describes how the DHRS connects to the steam and feed piping of the SGs to remove decay heat from the primary coolant, while Section B provides the general design basis and components of the reactor coolant system (RCS) that the DHRS interacts with, thus Section A specifies details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A details the materials and components of the pressurizer, which is a part of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) that Section B describes as being cooled by the DHRS in its design basis, thus connecting the pressurizer's composition to the overall DHRS diagram composition.
Section A provides specific material and component details of the pressurizer, which is one of the components generally described as part of the reactor coolant system in Section B.

Section A describes the DHRS as a subsystem designed to remove decay heat from the RCS, while Section B provides a general description of the RCS, of which the DHRS is a part, thus Section A specifies details for the system described in Section B.

Section A describes system components such as the RCS and its degasification and venting features, while Section B explains how the DHRS connects to steam and feed piping (including the SGs) to remove decay heat, thus Section A's description of system components provides the foundational elements that are depicted in the DHRS simplified diagram discussed in Section B.

Section A provides a general system description of the RCS and its degasification/venting functions, while Section B specifies the detailed components and materials of the pressurizer, which is part of the RCS described in Section A.

Section A describes components of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), some of which (like the degasification line and reactor vent valves) are relevant to the operation and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) described in Section B, thus providing subsystem details that are part of the DHRS simplified diagram's composition.

Section B provides a summary description and evaluation of the DHRS and its connections, which directly addresses the analysis question about the composition of the DHRS simplified diagram, while Section A is a preface that introduces the document containing Section B.

Section A is the preface of the document that provides context and copyright information, while Section B is a reference section detailing specific component materials, with Section A structurally preceding and encompassing Section B as part of the same document.

Section A provides the general context and copyright information for the NuScale Standard Plant documentation, while Section B presents specific technical details about the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) relevant to the analysis question about the DHRS simplified diagram's composition.

Section B explains that the DHRS is connected to the steam generator (SG) piping, while Section A provides detailed information about the design and characteristics of the reactor coolant system piping and SGs, which are components depicted in the DHRS simplified diagram.

Section A discusses the design and degradation mechanisms of reactor coolant system piping, while Section B details the materials and components of the pressurizer, both providing specific subsystem/component information that together contribute to understanding the composition of the DHRS simplified diagram.

Section A describes the reactor coolant system piping, which is a component that interfaces with the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) discussed in Section B, indicating that the composition of the DHRS simplified diagram would include elements from Section A as part of its system boundary.
